entry.title: Tutorial: Using the SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit App - Step-by-Step
entry.content:
Purpose:

This tutorial will guide you through the process of using the SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit app to migrate data to your S/4HANA system. The Migration Cockpit is a powerful, license-included tool designed for efficient data migration, especially when leveraging its pre-configured migration content.

Key Benefits of SAP Migration Cockpit:

  • S/4HANA Native App: Designed specifically for S/4HANA, replacing the older LTMC (Legacy Transfer Migration Cockpit) in S/4HANA OP (On-Premise) from version 2020 onwards. It is also the standard for S/4HANA Cloud.
  • Pre-configured Migration Content: Offers ready-to-use migration objects for various SAP business objects (e.g., Customers, Vendors, Materials, Business Partners, Financial Accounting data).
  • Automated Mapping: Provides pre-defined mapping between source data and target S/4HANA structures, significantly reducing manual mapping efforts.
  • Guided Migration Process: Provides a step-by-step approach, simplifying the migration workflow.
  • Data Validation and Simulation: Includes built-in data validation checks and simulation runs to identify potential issues before actual data migration.
  • License Included: Part of the standard S/4HANA license, no additional licensing cost.
  • Reduced Development Effort: Leveraging pre-configured content eliminates the need to build custom migration tools from scratch for many common migration scenarios.

Prerequisites:

  • SAP S/4HANA System Access: You need access to your SAP S/4HANA system (On-Premise or Cloud).
  • SAP Fiori Launchpad Access: Access to the SAP Fiori Launchpad to use the Migration Cockpit app.
  • Authorization: You need to have a user role assigned that grants you authorization to use the Migration Cockpit app and related data migration authorizations. Typically, roles related to "Data Migration" or "Business Configuration" are required.
  • Source Data: Your source data needs to be prepared in a supported format (e.g., CSV, XML, staging tables) based on the requirements of the chosen migration objects.

Step-by-Step Procedure:

Step 1: Access the Migration Cockpit Fiori App

  1. Log in to SAP Fiori Launchpad: Open your web browser and navigate to your SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad URL.

  2. Log in: Enter your SAP User ID and Password.

  3. Find the "Migration Cockpit" App:

    • Use the Fiori Launchpad search bar (usually at the top) and enter "Migration Cockpit".
    • Click on the tile named "Migration Cockpit" to open the application.

    (If you cannot find the app, ensure you have the necessary roles and authorizations assigned to your user. Contact your system administrator if needed.)

Step 2: Create a Migration Project

A Migration Project is a container to organize your migration activities.

  1. "Create" Button: In the Migration Cockpit app, click on the "Create" button to create a new migration project.

  2. Project Details: In the "Create Project" dialog box:

    • Project Name: Enter a meaningful name for your migration project (e.g., "Customer Master Migration - Go-Live", "Material Data Load - Initial Setup"). Choose a name that clearly indicates the scope of the migration.
    • Description (Optional): Enter a brief description of the project.
    • Mass Transfer ID (Automatically Generated): The system will automatically generate a Mass Transfer ID. This is a technical identifier for the project.
    • Click "Create".

  3. Project Created: The system will create the migration project, and you will be taken to the project overview screen.

Step 3: Select Migration Objects

Migration Objects are pre-defined templates for migrating specific types of data (e.g., Business Partner, Material, Customer, etc.).

  1. "Migration Objects" Tile: In your Migration Project overview, you will see a tile labeled "Migration Objects". Click on this tile to select the migration objects.

  2. Browse Migration Objects: The system will display a list of available Migration Objects, categorized by area (e.g., Business Partner, Finance, Logistics, Product).

    • Search and Filter: Use the search bar or filters to find the specific Migration Objects you need for your data migration. For example, search for "Customer", "Vendor", "Material", "G/L Account", etc.
    • Select Objects: Select the checkbox next to each Migration Object you want to include in your project.

  3. "OK" or "Add Objects" Button: Once you have selected all necessary Migration Objects, click "OK" or a button like "Add Objects to Project" to add them to your migration project.

  4. Migration Objects Added to Project: The selected Migration Objects will now be listed within your Migration Project overview.

Step 4: Download Migration Template (for Selected Object)

For each Migration Object you've added, you need to download the migration template. This template is typically a Microsoft Excel file (XLSX) or CSV file with predefined columns that correspond to the target fields in SAP S/4HANA.

  1. Select Migration Object: In your project overview, click on the Migration Object for which you want to download the template (e.g., "Business Partner").

  2. "Download File Template" Button: On the Migration Object details screen, look for and click the "Download File Template" button.

  3. Choose Template Type (if prompted): You might be prompted to choose between different template types (e.g., XLSX, CSV). Select the desired format (XLSX is generally recommended for ease of use).

  4. Save Template File: Save the downloaded template file (e.g., Excel file) to your local computer.

Step 5: Populate the Migration Template with Source Data

  1. Open the Template File: Open the downloaded template file (e.g., Excel file) using Microsoft Excel or a compatible spreadsheet application.

  2. Understand Template Structure: Review the structure of the template. It will typically have:

    • Worksheets: Multiple worksheets might exist for different parts of the Migration Object (e.g., one for general data, one for addresses, one for bank details for Business Partner).
    • Columns: Each column header in the worksheet represents a field in the target SAP S/4HANA data structure. Pay close attention to the column headers and instructions in the template. Some columns might be mandatory, some optional. Data types and formats might be specified (e.g., date format, numeric format). Read any "Instructions" or "Information" tabs in the template.

  3. Populate with Source Data: Carefully copy or enter your source data into the corresponding columns of the template.

    • Data Transformation (if needed): You might need to perform data transformation or cleansing in your source data or within the template to ensure it matches the expected format and values in SAP S/4HANA. For example, date format conversions, value mapping (e.g., mapping source country codes to SAP country codes), etc.
    • Mandatory Fields: Ensure you provide data for all mandatory fields (usually indicated in the template or documentation).
    • Data Validation: As you populate the data, consider performing basic data validation to catch errors early (e.g., data type checks, format checks, value range checks).

  4. Save the Populated Template: Save the populated template file.

Step 6: Upload the Data to the Migration Cockpit

  1. Go back to Migration Cockpit App: Return to the "Migration Cockpit" Fiori app and your Migration Project and selected Migration Object.

  2. "Upload File" Button: On the Migration Object details screen, look for and click the "Upload File" button (or a similar button for uploading data).

  3. Select Data File: In the "Upload File" dialog box, browse to and select the populated template file (e.g., Excel file) from your local computer.

  4. Upload and Parsing: Click "Upload" or "Open". The Migration Cockpit will upload the file and start parsing the data from the file. You will see progress indicators.

  5. Data Validation: The Migration Cockpit performs automatic data validation checks based on the pre-defined rules for the Migration Object.

  6. Review Validation Results: After parsing and validation, the app will display the validation results.

    • Successes: Records that passed validation without errors.
    • Errors: Records that failed validation and need correction.
    • Warnings: Records with potential issues that might require review (but may still be processed).
    • Download Error Log (if errors exist): If there are errors, download the error log file. This log file will provide details about the errors encountered for each record.

  7. Correct Data Errors (if any):

    • Analyze Error Log: Open the error log file and carefully analyze the error messages. They will typically indicate which fields have errors and what the issues are (e.g., incorrect data type, invalid value, missing mandatory field).
    • Correct Data in Template: Go back to your populated template file, locate the records with errors (usually identified by record number/row number in the error log), and correct the data in the template based on the error messages.
    • Re-upload Corrected File: After correcting the data, save the corrected template file and re-upload the corrected file to the Migration Cockpit (repeat Step 6). Repeat this process of error analysis, correction, and re-upload until you have resolved all critical errors and ideally warnings as well.

Step 7: Simulate Migration (Highly Recommended)

Before performing the actual data migration, it's strongly recommended to run a Simulation. This simulates the data migration process without actually creating or changing data in your productive SAP S/4HANA system. It helps you identify potential issues that might not be caught by data validation alone.

  1. "Simulate" Button: On the Migration Object details screen, click the "Simulate" button (or a similar button for simulation).

  2. Simulation Run: The Migration Cockpit will perform a simulation run using your uploaded data and the selected Migration Object. This might take some time depending on the data volume and object complexity.

  3. Review Simulation Results: After the simulation completes, review the simulation results.

    • Successes: Records that would be successfully migrated in a real run.
    • Errors (Simulation Errors): Simulation might identify further errors that were not caught in the initial validation. These errors must be addressed before the actual migration.
    • Warnings (Simulation Warnings): Review warnings. They might indicate potential data quality issues or areas for improvement.
    • Download Simulation Log (if errors or warnings): If there are simulation errors or warnings, download the simulation log to get details and analyze the issues.

  4. Correct Data/Configuration (if needed): If the simulation reveals errors, you will need to:

    • Analyze Simulation Errors: Analyze the simulation error log.
    • Correct Data in Template OR Configuration: The errors might be due to data issues in your template that need correction (repeat Step 6 error correction process). Or, in more complex cases, the simulation might highlight configuration gaps or issues in your SAP S/4HANA system that need to be addressed by your SAP configuration team.
    • Re-simulate: After making corrections, re-simulate the migration to confirm the issues are resolved.

Step 8: Migrate Data (Execute Migration)

Once you have successfully simulated the migration and resolved any errors, you can proceed with the actual data migration (Execute Migration). This step will create or update data in your SAP S/4HANA system.

  1. "Migrate" Button (or "Execute Migration"): On the Migration Object details screen, click the "Migrate" button (or a similar button like "Execute Migration"). Be absolutely sure you are ready to migrate real data before clicking this button!

  2. Confirmation Prompt: The system might display a confirmation prompt asking if you are sure you want to proceed with the migration. Confirm only if you are ready.

  3. Data Migration Run: The Migration Cockpit will execute the data migration process. This might take a significant amount of time depending on the volume of data and the complexity of the Migration Objects. You will see progress indicators.

  4. Migration Results: After the migration run completes, review the migration results.

    • Successes: Records that were successfully migrated to SAP S/4HANA.
    • Errors (Migration Errors): If there are any errors during the migration run, the app will report them. Migration errors need immediate attention.
    • Warnings (Migration Warnings): Review warnings.

  5. Download Migration Log (if errors or for audit): Download the migration log file. This log contains detailed information about the migration run, including successes, errors, warnings, and processing steps. Keep this log for audit purposes and error analysis.

  6. Handle Migration Errors (if any):

    • Analyze Migration Error Log: If there are migration errors, download and analyze the error log file.
    • Correct Data or Configuration: Identify the root cause of the errors. This might require data correction in your template, or it could indicate configuration issues in SAP S/4HANA that need to be addressed by your SAP configuration team.
    • Re-migrate (Corrected Data): After correcting the issues, you might need to re-upload corrected data (if data errors) and re-run the migration process for the failed records. The Migration Cockpit often allows you to re-migrate only the failed records, rather than re-migrating the entire file.

Step 9: Post-Migration Activities (Data Validation and Reconciliation)

After the Migration Cockpit reports successful migration, it's essential to perform post-migration data validation and reconciliation to ensure data accuracy and completeness in your SAP S/4HANA system.

  1. Data Validation: Verify that the migrated data is correctly created or updated in SAP S/4HANA as expected.

    • Sample Checks: Perform sample checks in SAP S/4HANA using relevant Fiori apps or SAP GUI transactions to review migrated data (e.g., display Business Partners, Materials, Customers, etc.).
    • Reporting: Run reports in SAP S/4HANA to compare data before and after migration to verify data completeness and accuracy.
    • Business User Verification: Involve business users to review migrated data in their respective areas to ensure it meets their requirements.

  2. Data Reconciliation: Reconcile the migrated data against your source data to ensure that all intended data has been migrated and that there are no data losses or inconsistencies.

  3. Document Migration Results: Document the entire migration process, including:

    • Migration Project Name and Mass Transfer ID.
    • Migration Objects used.
    • Source data files and templates.
    • Validation and simulation results.
    • Migration execution logs.
    • Data validation and reconciliation results.
    • Any issues encountered and their resolutions.

Summary:

The SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit app is a powerful and efficient tool for data migration, especially for standard business objects. By following these steps – creating a project, selecting migration objects, preparing data templates, uploading, validating, simulating, migrating, and verifying – you can effectively migrate data to your S/4HANA system using the pre-configured content and guided process provided by the Migration Cockpit. Remember to pay close attention to data quality, validation results, and error handling throughout the migration process.

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mickaelquesnot_gusap-s4-hanamigration-cockpit-app-step-activity-7292063808523567104-LPfA?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJPDL8BjpJe3thZB6t04k7Z2hRVHhboAec

#SAP

entry.title: SAP IBP Configuration - Create a Key Figure Using an Attribute in SAP IBP
entry.content:
In SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP), attributes as key figures allow you to use attributes from master data types as key figures in your planning models. This feature provides flexibility and enhances the granularity of your planning data. Here are some key points:

  • Definition: Attributes from master data types (e.g., product, customer) can be defined as key figures. For example, the unit price of a product can be used as a key figure.
  • Usage: This approach is useful for scenarios where you need to use specific attribute values in calculations or planning processes.
  • Configuration: You can configure attributes as key figures in the Planning Areas app within SAP IBP.

This functionality helps in creating more detailed and accurate planning models by leveraging existing master data attributes.

1. In the Model Configuration group, choose the Planning Areas Model Configuration tile.

2. Choose your planning area in the list, and choose the Attributes as Key Figures tab.

 

3. Choose New to create attributes as key figure.

 

4. In the Attributes as Key Figures dialog appears, enter Attribute ID, Base Planning level, From Period, and To Period.

From Period

If you want to upload the key figure value for a range of periods, enter values relative to the current period in the From Period and To Period fields.

If you enter 0 in the From Period field, the system takes the current period as the From Period.

If you leave the From Period and To Period fields blank and don’t specify a Time Reference Attribute either, the attribute value is stored in the database for all the time periods that are available based on the time profile of the planning area, which should be avoided for performance reasons.

Time Reference Attribute

Select a time reference attribute if you want to upload the key figure value for a single period. The time reference attribute is an attribute of the timestamp data type and comes from the same master data type as the attribute as key figure.

5. Choose Save to save key figure.

6. Edit the key figure created from attributes. Choose key figure tab and select key figure, choose Edit.

7. Change parameters in key figure page.

Proportionality

If you have specified a disaggregation mode (either Equal Distribution or Copy Value), you can use this field to define the proportionality for disaggregation. You have the following options:

ValueDescription

No Proportional Disaggregation

Values are disaggregated according to the disaggregation mode.

Same Key Figure – Stored Values

If the stored values of the same key figure are not 0, the aggregated values are disaggregated proportional to them, otherwise according to the disaggregation mode.

Same Key Figure – Calculated Values

If the calculated values of the same key figure are not 0, the aggregated values are disaggregated proportional to them, otherwise according to the disaggregation mode. In this case, a disaggregation expression is generated based on the calculation rules during activation.

Other Key Figure – Stored Values

If the stored values of the key figure specified in the Key Figure for Proportionality field are not 0, the aggregated values are disaggregated proportional to them, otherwise according to the disaggregation mode.

Disaggregation Expression

If the resulting values of the disaggregation expression are not 0, the aggregated values are disaggregated proportional to them, otherwise, according to the disaggregation mode.

For information about how the various combinations of those values correspond to combinations of values for the relevant fields in the Configuration app, see Configuration of Proportional Disaggregation in the Model Configuration Guide on SAP Help Portal at http://help.sap.com/ibp.

entry.title: Router Pallet in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Router:
A router works based on the conditions provided. It determines what data needs to reach the target or        receiver. When we use a router palette, we need to provide conditions for the router. Based on these          conditions, the process occurs, and the data reaches the receiver.

"In a router, we have two parts:

  1. General: In the General section, if desired, we can assign meaningful names.
  2. Processing: In the Processing section, the actual processing occurs. This is where we define the conditions.

In the Processing section, there are several fields.

  • Expression Type: Here, we need to specify the type, whether it is XML or non-XML,
  • Condition: Here we define the condition for the process.
  • Default: we use this to send the default data, when the conditions are not satisfying the given condition then default option comes to play.

 

 

In content modifier we need to define header for which we will used to for the non-XML condition.

 

For Expression Type of Non-XML, the condition should be like ${header. <Header Name} = ‘value’

For other references you can refer this link for more non-xml conditions Define Router | SAP Help Portal

 

For Expression Type of XML, the condition should be in like

/ PersonalDetails/ PersonalDetail/ PersonalID = ‘9652’

 

First condition output:

 

Second condition output.

 


Third one is default:

 

thank you, if you find any miss information, let me know in comments...
#sapcpi #Router #integration suite

Re: Make invisible Purchasing Group in ME51N
entry.content:
Hello @athakyriaz 

SAP has delivered a similar functionality for sales organizations - see the note 3389687 - How to hide SD organizational data in the F4 help for VA* transaction but not for purchasing groups according to my knowledge.    

A functionality to hide obsolete customizing values is available in the Defense Forces & Public Security industry solution component - see SAP Help: Hiding of Invalid Entries from Input Help Obsolete values are defined in the OBSOLETECUST, which need to be activated with BS_OBSOLETE_CUST_SFWS. I'm not quite sure if the functionality to hide obsolete values can be used outside of the Defense Forces & Public Security industry solution component and I'm quite sure that the component is licensed additionally. 

The implementation is quite straightforward with a search help exit:

If you don't want or can't use SAP standard here, I guess you could implement a similar exit to hide unwanted purchase groups.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Product description not showing up in third-party PO whereas it is showing up in preceding SO
entry.content:
The third-party purchase order created automatically from sales order by API cannot show product description in traditional Chinese (ZF).

The description can only displayed in EN, if there is EN description maintained.

However the description stay empty when there is only ZF description maintained in the master data.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Product description not showing up in third-party PO...
entry.content:
The Product Description displayed is not retrieved from the Product Master Data at run time. Instead, the description is copied from the Product Master to the Purchase Order Item during the creation of the purchase order in the language in which you are logged in.

The description will be displayed based on Logon language and respective description maintained in the master data at the time of creation of Purchase request/ order.

For example, if the product description in master data is maintained in language ZF, but the user who created this purchase request was logged in with EN by default via soap UI, as a result when the Purchase request/ order was created, system looked for the product description in that logon language EN and if there was no product description maintained in that language, it saved the purchase request/order with blank product description. That's why when you log in EN to check, you can see the product description in EN. However description cannot be seen when log in ZF.

To ensure the product description shown in ZF during the first creation of purchase order, please set the log on language of technical user in SOAP UI following this KBA steps: 2951384 - How to change Log on language of Technical users uploading documents via Soap UI

Besides, in order to make sure the output of the order to be displayed as ZF as you expected, you also need to maintain the language of the supplier on master data level, please follow the steps in this KBA: 3407286 - Changed Purchas Order via SOAP UI Output Template in Unexpected Language 

Please also check this KBA that may help you to understand the system behavior: 2735797 - Product Description appearing empty or wrong in any purchasing documents

entry.title: Store and Use Token API Response for Authentication in SAP CPI
entry.content:
In the modern API landscape, many services require token-based authentication. These tokens often have a limited lifespan, which means they need to be refreshed periodically to maintain seamless access to APIs. SAP Cloud Platform Integration (SAP CPI) offers a streamlined way to handle such authentication mechanisms, enabling you to automate the retrieval and usage of tokens in your integration flows.

In this blog, we will walk you through the steps of retrieving a token from a Token API, storing it in a variable, and using it to authenticate subsequent API requests. By the end of this article, you’ll be equipped with the knowledge to implement a token-based authentication system in your SAP CPI iFlow.

1. Retrieve a Token from a Token API

To begin, we need to consume the Token API. This API provides the authentication token that we will store for future use. Here's how to retrieve the token:

Step 1: Use the Start Timer

Start by using the Start Timer component in your iFlow. This will allow the token API to be called at regular intervals, ensuring the token is always refreshed when required.

  • Add a Start Timer to your iFlow and configure it according to the refresh interval required by your Token API.

Step 2: Use Request-Reply to Call the API

After the Start Timer, use the Request-Reply pattern to send a request to the Token API.

  • In the Request component, select the GET method to fetch the token.
  • Map the API endpoint and any required authentication headers or parameters.

 

Step 3: Convert JSON to XML (if necessary)

If the Token API returns a response in JSON format, you will need to convert it into XML for further processing in SAP CPI.

  • Use the JSON to XML Converter to convert the response from JSON to XML.
  • If the response is already in XML format, skip this step.

 

2. Store the Token in a Variable

Once you have received the token response, it’s time to store it in a variable for easy access later in your iFlow.

Step 1: Add Write Variable

Next, add the Write Variable component to store the token.

  • In the Write Variable component, provide a suitable name for the variable (e.g., authToken).
  • Set the Type as XPath.
  • Choose the Data Type as java.lang.String.
  • In the Value field, write an XPath expression to extract the token from the API response (e.g., //Token).

Ensure that you select the Global Scope checkbox so that the variable can be accessed across different parts of your iFlow.

Step 2: End the Flow

After storing the token, connect the Write Variable component to the End component to finalize the flow.

  • Deploy your iFlow, and the token will be stored and ready for subsequent use.

 

3. Use the Token for Authenticating Subsequent API Calls

Now that the token is stored in a global variable, we can use it to authenticate subsequent API calls in the iFlow.

Step 1: Add Content Modifier

To use the stored token in your API requests, add a Content Modifier to your iFlow.

  • In the Content Modifier, add a new header.
  • Set the Header Name to the appropriate authentication header.
  • Set the Source Type to Global Variable.
  • In the Source field, specify the name of the variable where the token is stored.

This will attach the token to the header of your outgoing requests, allowing the target API to authenticate using the token.

By following these steps, you’ve automated the process of retrieving, storing, and using a token for authentication in SAP CPI. The token is periodically refreshed through the Start Timer and can be used for authenticating subsequent API calls via the Content Modifier. This ensures seamless, continuous integration without the need for manual token updates.

With this approach, you’ve built a robust authentication system for your integration flows, ensuring that your integrations remain secure and uninterrupted.

Happy Integration!

entry.title: Re: BDC for ME01
entry.content: Dear Sir,
